Assignment 1 Checking Code

Uwe F Mayer
Mentor

3 years ago

Fundamentally there are several ways to get this wrong:

  1. Incorrect parsing

  2. Incorrect indexing

  3. Incorrect sorting

  4. Modifying a provided global object such as df

  5. Defining a function inside of an assigned function (this is known to break the autograder). There is no check for this here.

That's why I provided several checks, you should run all checks even if one of them fails. These checks are intended for solutions that are mostly correct. If your solution is very incorrect this checking code might not help you much. As stated, do not modify df, if you do the autograder will likely fail to grade your submission and you might get a feedback message that makes no sense. If you need to add columns to df then make a copy first with something like mydf = df.copy() and then work with mydf.

This is how the correct solution looks like:

You should make sure your solution matches the output above, and only after it matches use the checking code provided in this discussion.

    """
    try:
        assert type(df) == pd.Series
        assert (df.index == pd.RangeIndex(start=0, stop=500, step=1)).all()
        assert (df.apply(type) == str).all()
        assert df.str.len().min() >= 6
        assert df.str[5].apply(ord).sum() == 38354
        print("Passed df modification check")
    except:
        print("Failed df modification check")
 
run_df_modified_check()
 
# check if running the code twice produces the same result
try:
    assert (date_sorter() == s_test).all()
    print("Passed repeatability check")
except:
    print("Failed repeatability check")
 
# check if the result has the expected index
try:
    # assert type(date_sorter().index) == pd.RangeIndex
    # assert (date_sorter().index == pd.RangeIndex(start=0, stop=500, step=1)).all()
    assert list(date_sorter().index) == list(range(500))
    print("Passed index check")
except:
    print("Failed index check")
 
# check the tie-break sort for a sample of records where some have the same date
# note that this only tests a sample and does not check the entire answer
try:
    test_indices = [335, 415, 323, 405, 370, 382, 303, 488, 283,
    """
    Check if df appears to be modified.
s_test = date_sorter()
 
def run_df_modified_check():
import re
import numpy as np
Enter to Rename, Shift+Enter to Preview

Information:
Pressing Tab in the current editor will insert the tab character. Toggle this behavior by pressing Control+M.

Check all the output blocks that are shown as incorrect according to the values check, all the others should be correct. At least one of the output blocks that fail the value checksum will have at least one incorrectly parsed date, or you sorted the records incorrectly, or both. Note that it is possible that the checking code says that an output block has an incorrect value checksum but all the dates that are in your output block are correct. This case will arise if a record that should be in that output block is not in the block because it's incorrectly parsed or incorrectly sorted, causing the output block to fail the value checksum because there is a record missing in that output block. That record will be then in some other block and will cause that other block to fail the values checksum as well.

Specifically, if your output has these records in output index position 19 and 20, then you are doing the secondary sort incorrectly:

That's the wrong sort order because if the dates are the same for two or more records then the record(s) with the lower original index should come first per the instructions. This means that in the output the record with input index (zero-based original row number) 323 should come before the one with input index 405.

There are two ways of doing this sorting.

  1. Sort the data by the parsed date column using a stable sort algorithm. "stable" is a technical term meaning that in case of any identical values in the sort column the original order will be preserved, and that's exactly what is required here. Pandas sort by a single column has an optional parameter kind="stable" that does just that.

  2. Alternatively, put the original row number into a column with reset_index() and then sort by the two columns parsed date and index.

As to the "secondary sort check", this check is only meaningful if the dates are correctly extracted, so if your parsing is wrong this check might fail even if your sorting approach is correct. If you need more explanation on what "secondary sort" means, then you might want to search the internet for "What is primary sort and secondary sort?"

Finally, double check that

  • your notebook does not in any way modify the provided object df

  • your notebook has only one definition of the function date_sorter, and it has a return statement returning a Series

  • you do not try to run any shell commands (such as !ls)

  • you do not try to run any macros (that's commands starting with %)

  • you do not try to import any libraries / packages the autograder doesn't have

  • the only place were you modified your notebook is inside the function body of date_sorter (if not, then rename your notebook, get a fresh version, and copy your code over from the old to the new one, see How to reset an assignment in the general course discussion forum)

[The checking code was last updated 2024-03-08. Replies below might refer to older versions of the checking code.]

    Hi,

    I have an issue with the secondary sort according to the check, but if I look at your solution above, I see you just order by date ascending and use the ranking as the index. I sorted by date ascending, ranked and then sorted by the original index. That's how I understand the assignment based on the example shown there.

    Actions for Veerle De Saedeleer's post
    • Uwe F Mayer
      Mentor

      5 months ago

      About " Secondly I split up my code into functions which are outside of the main function body of date_sorter () , but inside the same cell. Could that cause issues as well?"

      Answer: It should not, but it might, if you use some global variables that collapse with variables defined inside of date_sorter. Simply move all the functions into date_sorter, and if they use variables external to their definition (a bad coding practice in the first place), then make sure you define the functions after you set those variables.

      About sorting, there's a difference between sorting by two columns in one sort operation, and sorting by two columns in two sorting operations. For example, [(1,2), (1, 3), (2, 1)] is sorted if you sort in one operation by the first column, with tie-break by the second column. However, if you sort first by the first column, and then sort again by the second column, then you get [(2, 1), (1,2), (1, 3)], which is obviously not the same thing. If you have a dataframe D with two columns C1 and C2, you can easily sort with one operation by C1 with tie-break by C2 with D.sort_values(['C1', 'C2']).

      Actions for Uwe F Mayer's post

    Hello, My output seems to be ordered correctly but I keep failing the modification check. Is there a way for me to re-download the original data? Can you please help me?

    Actions for xanthos kekkos's post
    • Uwe F Mayer
      Mentor

      2 years ago

      The modification check does not check if your local data is modified. If your code passes the value check then your data is ok. The modification check is to find out if you modified the provided df object, because if you do the autograder will likely fail your submission. The solution to this is very easy, simply add the line mydf=df.copy() and work with mydf.

  • ENEhsan Nikfarjam
    Learner

    2 years ago

    Hello Uwe,

    I hope this message finds you well. I have a question about this assignment and would really appreciate your insights.

    I've noticed a discrepancy in the behavior of my code when running it in Visual Studio versus Jupyter Notebook. In Visual Studio, the code produces the expected results and passes the checking code. However, in Jupyter Notebook, there are some differences, especially with the correct replacement of misspelled words. It seems like the replacements aren't aligning as intended.

    I've attached a brief explanation of my code for your consideration. Additionally, I've included screenshots of the results in both Visual Studio and Jupyter Notebook to help illustrate the issue.

    If you could spare a moment to provide some guidance on why there might be this variation between the two platforms or if there are any specific considerations or adjustments I should be aware of for better cross-platform consistency, I would greatly appreciate your expertise.

    Thank you for your time and assistance. Looking forward to your feedback.

    **** A brief explanation of the code:

    1. Reading the Text File

    • This code opens a file named 'dates.txt' and reads its contents line by line.

    • The lines are then stored in a pandas Series called “df” with the name 'text'.

    2. Mapping Dictionary for Correcting Misspelled Month Names

    • A dictionary month_mapping is created to store misspelled month names as keys and their correct spellings as values.

    3. Correcting Misspelled Month Names in the Text

    • The misspelled month names in the text are replaced with their correct spellings using the str.replace method.

    4. Regex Patterns for Different Date Formats

    • Regular expressions (regex) patterns are defined to match various date formats.

    • Each pattern is designed to capture different ways dates might be written.

    5. Combining Regex Patterns

    • All the defined patterns are combined into a single regex pattern using the | (OR) operator.

    6. Extracting Dates Using Regex

    • The str.extract method is used to find matches for the combined regex pattern in the text.

    • The flags=re.IGNORECASE parameter makes the matching case-insensitive.

    7. Printing Extracted Dates with Line Numbers

    • The extracted dates along with their line numbers are printed to the console.

    8. Parsing and Normalizing Dates

    • The pd.to_datetime function is used to parse the extracted dates into datetime objects.

    • Dates that cannot be parsed are set to NaT (Not a Time) using errors='coerce'.

    9. Adjusting Two-Digit Years

    • Two-digit years are adjusted by subtracting 100 years if the year is greater than 2023.

    10. Sorting Indices Based on Parsed Dates

    • The indices are sorted based on the parsed dates using the 'mergesort' algorithm.

    11. Returning the Result

    • The sorted indices are returned as a pandas Series with the name 'index'.

    12. Calling the Function and Printing the Result

    • The date_sorter function is called, and the result (sorted indices) is printed to the console.

    **** The results in Visual Studio:

    **** The result in Jupyter Notebook:

    Please let me know if you need more info! Many thanks.

    • Uwe F Mayer
      Mentor

      2 years ago

      Your screenshot in black is incomplete to fully see what's wrong. You also need to look at records with output numbers 330-339, as that's the last block that got a "False" in the check code. Very likely there' a record in your output block 330-339 that should be in the 260-269 output block. Having one such incorrectly parsed record will shift all records in between by one position, and hence creating a sequence of "False" in the value check code from block 260 all the way to and including block 330.
